chapters.title: The Anthropocene age is a sharp tool with two edges
chapters.text:
When Homo sapiens first appeared on Earth, the planet barely noticed. Human activity was a small drop in a vast ocean, leaving no real mark on the planet’s story. Today the picture looks very different. Weigh all wild mammals and birds, then compare that weight to human mass. The ratio is under a hundred million to roughly three hundred and eighty-five million tons. Humans have become the dominant species in a literal sense.

We shape our lives and everything else on Earth, from the ocean floor to the upper air. Yet this power is full of contradictions.

Some people spend their holidays helping baby sea turtles reach the ocean. Others pour fresh water onto lawns they never walk on. Humanity built the internet and landed on the moon, and also drove the dodo, Steller’s sea cow, and many other species to extinction. Our capitalist world gives us fine appliances and yet cannot wipe out poverty.

We live in an age when the survival of many species rests on our choices.

It is hard to say whether the harms of our actions outweigh the gains, but they clearly shape the planet’s path. That is why some call the current age the Anthropocene. Our leading role, however, does not guarantee that we will thrive in it. Ecological and economic pressures, like scarce resources, can set off irreversible changes. To steer clear of the worst, we have to look at the root causes, the features of our societies. Some of them are alarming, yet there is also a thin line of hope.

Caring for the environment is only one piece of the Anthropocene that we have to think about. John Green reflects on other pieces too and invites you to join him. Are you hungry for food for thought? If so, let us begin.

chapters.title: The Monopoly story has been read the wrong way
chapters.text:
When Green watches car races, he often thinks about what drives the sport. Some would say the speed, but speed is just a tool. The real engine is a wish to beat the rest and finish first. That wish can fuel stunning feats, yet moving fast only helps if the direction is right. Fitzgerald’s Gatsby shows what happens when multiplying money becomes the only point of life. We like to think we are immune to the win-at-all-costs mindset and always play fair. The story of Monopoly tells a different story.

In the rush to win, we can lose sight of whether the prize is worth the chase.

Ask people about the rules of Monopoly and they will likely say, buy all the property and bankrupt everyone else. Yet there was a time when that was only one of two ways to play.

A writer named Elizabeth Magie patented The Landlord’s Game in 1904. A short history explains what she was after. As a feminist, she wanted to draw attention to the unfair treatment of women and clear a path toward gender equality. At first she tried a bold move. She placed an ad in a newspaper offering to sell herself to the highest bidder. The story made the news, which let Magie share her belief that women are not property and men should not treat them that way.

She soon dug deeper and saw that the economic system itself worked against women. So she designed a board game with two sets of rules. One set looked like the Monopoly we know. The other set encouraged players to earn and thrive together. Magie hoped that players would see the value in the second set and carry that idea into real life.

Time proved that hope was too high. Players preferred the first set. The game spread fast, and the win-together rules got lost in later versions. Monopoly became a song of capitalism, the opposite of what Magie had hoped for. Yet a system where only one wins and others lose means the whole society fails, because it cannot bring in and use everyone’s talents.

chapters.title: Our habits with food and energy deserve a hard look
chapters.text:
We can read people by watching their habits. The same goes for traditions and whole societies. Among American customs, Green singles out Nathan’s Hot Dog Eating Contest. Each year on July 4, crowds gather in Brooklyn to watch a new kind of sport, speed eating. Contestants try to eat as many hot dogs and buns as possible in ten minutes. The event grew so famous that similar contests now pop up on Independence Day.

For Green, the contest is a symbol of overdoing it. People eat more than they want, let alone more than they need. The contests also show what people eat. Americans get almost two thirds of their daily calories from highly processed food. Where did that habit start? A short history of how processed food became so common helps explain it.

In the early twentieth century, corner stores full of fresh produce were the main place to shop. Grocers picked out items for the customer. In 1916, Clarence Saunders broke that pattern with a new kind of store built on self-service. He called it Piggly Wiggly. Shoppers could wander down dozens of aisles and pick their own products. The store needed fewer clerks and did not sell on credit, so prices stayed low. Another trick was stretching the expiration date. That is how prepackaged and processed foods reached the shelves as a cheap option for low-income shoppers. As Piggly Wiggly grew, so did the makers of prepared food.

The launch of self-service stores in 1916 lined up with the boom in advertising.

The Anthropocene’s overdoing it goes beyond food. It shapes how we treat resources in general. Air conditioners, or ACs, show the point. They are vital in hot regions, and pharmacies depend on them. Many medications, from antibiotics to insulin, must be stored at twenty to twenty-five degrees Celsius.

Still, companies often run cooling harder than they need to, sometimes without knowing it. The reason is that the standard office temperature, often called optimal, is set for the average worker. In most studies, the average worker is a man in a suit. Is it not strange that we overcool offices so men can keep their jackets on? A few degrees warmer saves electricity and boosts focus. A Cornell University study found that raising the office temperature from twenty to twenty-five degrees Celsius cut typing errors by almost half.

For reference, AC units use about ten percent of the world’s electricity.

chapters.title: The pictures we see rarely match what is real
chapters.text:
For many of us, the velociraptors in Jurassic Park became the model of what a dinosaur looks like. Shown as huge, scaly, and fierce, they played a big role in the movies. Real velociraptors, however, were feathered, about the size of a turkey, had long tails, and lived in the area we now call Mongolia. How did the gap appear? The author of Jurassic Park, Michael Crichton, actually described a different dinosaur called deinonychus in his story. Its name did not sound scary enough, so he swapped in the name velociraptor.

Similar gaps show up in places beyond fiction. The story of White Wilderness proves that documentaries are not safe from it. The film won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature in 1958 and told a story about Arctic plants and animals. There is a catch. In one scene, lemmings, small tundra rodents, leap off a cliff into the Arctic Ocean during their migration. Some see it as a brave fight against nature, while others see the animals as foolish. The real reason was human. The crew pushed them into the water and filmed them fall and drown. That is only one of several fakes in the film.

Misrepresentation can also happen by accident. Even when news journalists try to be fair, the format itself tilts the picture.

News focuses on recent events, not always on the most important ones. Media reports on actions, not on slow processes, so you are unlikely to hear that the global child mortality rate has dropped by sixty percent in the last thirty years. The short length of news reports leaves no room for background, and background is often the key to understanding.

What you say matters when you have the microphone, even when you are joking, as John Green has noted.

Sometimes the gap between image and reality is huge. Green has a story that shows it. For a while, he shared a flat with three friends named Katie, Shannon, and Hassan. Hassan came from Kuwait but had relatives in Iraq. He was nervous about his family when the United States invaded Iraq in 2003. The only window he had was the news, and he watched every report he could. Once, he and Green were watching a piece from Baghdad. The reporters talked about anger in the streets. Hassan smiled. Behind them, Arabic graffiti read, Happy birthday, sir, despite the circumstances.

Reality is always more layered than the way it is shown.

chapters.title: Hidden helpers build the breakthroughs we celebrate
chapters.text:
In the Anthropocene, we often split humanity into geniuses and the rest. The media loves to report breakthroughs and the few people who made them. They deserve credit, but so do the people whose ideas gave them a base to stand on. Green believes we should give more airtime to those hidden helpers, the social systems and communities that support big ideas. A few historical examples make the point.

Halley’s comet, the only periodic comet we know by name, owes its name to English astronomer Edmond Halley. He figured out that it returns every seventy-four to seventy-nine years and predicted that it would appear again in 1758. He was also well known for his work on Earth’s magnetic field and the orbit of the moon.

Halley stood on the shoulders of a community. He studied reports from other astronomers who had seen similar comets in 1607 and 1531. Before his prediction, Robert Hooke, a star in many fields, had already suggested that some comets return. The Royal Society and other groups gave him the right setting to do the work.

The same pattern holds across the centuries. What if Michelangelo had not lived in powerful and wealthy Florence? What would he have done without new ideas in anatomy that made his art so exact? The Sistine Chapel ceiling was not only his. He had gifted helpers. Thomas Edison worked with a team in the nineteenth century to make his inventions.

The brightest minds rarely work alone.

Stories of lone geniuses skip these details because they are messy. Skipping them does not shrink the role of talent. Noticing the helpers builds appreciation for those behind the curtain.

That is why Green thinks art is a bit like the painted ball in Alexandria, Indiana. Its owner, Mike Carmichael, started the project in 1977. Day after day, his family added fresh coats of paint. Soon the ball drew visitors, who now do most of the painting. The ball is fourteen feet around and has more than twenty-six thousand layers of paint. We cannot see most of the layers, yet each one is part of the whole. For Green, the ball is a symbol that each of us, no matter how small our part, slowly shapes what art becomes. Every action matters.

chapters.title: Power and responsibility should always travel together
chapters.text:
After he says good night to his children, Green often hears them say, polar bears. They remind him to turn off the lights to save electricity. Their teacher told them that turning off lights can save animals, including polar bears, from extinction.

That fear that something can vanish because of what we do is a thin line of hope inside the Anthropocene, a feeling of responsibility. That feeling has already saved, for example, Canada geese. Scientists once thought the species would be gone by the middle of the twentieth century because of hunting. Hunters would catch one goose, cripple its wings, and leave it in a field to call others. When the flock came to the decoy, the hunt began. That practice was made illegal in 1935, which saved the geese. Year by year, their numbers slowly came back from the damage people had done.

Another example comes from the autumn of 1940. A teenager named Marcel Ravidat was walking with his dog near Montignac in France. The dog vanished into a hole, and Marcel found a hidden passage. Believing the path led to Lascaux Manor, he and three friends went in and discovered a cave.

Instead of gold and gems, they found another kind of treasure, striking paintings of animals. The art was so powerful that Pablo Picasso reportedly marveled at it. Even though they were young, the boys knew they had to protect the paintings. Not all of them could. Georges Agniel had to go back to the capital for his studies, and Simon Coencas, who was Jewish and hiding from the Nazis, had to flee. So the other two, Jacques Marsal and Marcel Ravidat, guarded the cave nonstop until officials put a door at the entrance. After the Second World War, the boys became guides to the cave.

Even the darkest times cannot kill the responsibility we feel for the planet’s heritage.

The boys’ dedication to a cave with no chests of treasure is not the only fact that amazes Green. What did officials do when the breath of too many visitors changed the cave’s climate and put the paintings at risk? They could have ignored the danger. Instead, they closed the cave to the public in 1963 and built replica caves, like Lascaux II, where visitors can see copies of the artwork.

chapters.title: Hope is a strong cure for worry about what comes next
chapters.text:
Some argue that the small good deeds we do barely move the needle next to the size of the problems. Rising seas, vanishing forests, the gap between rich and poor, and risks from new technology can feel crushing. The question of how to handle it all pops into our heads and pulls us toward despair. We do not have time for that feeling, because it blocks the change we need. Instead, we should look for fresh angles and fill our lives with new meaning that gives us hope. We choose how we see the world. Green learned that from his own life.

In 2007, his wife got a job at the Indianapolis Museum of Art, so the couple moved there. When Green arrived, he could not wait to leave. He dreamed of a new job offer that would take his wife somewhere else. The rows of office buildings, the jumble of small stores, and the weak public transport system did not charm him. He thought the city should invest more in art or something else to give it a spark. How did locals put up with the name Ditch Road for one of the main streets, he wondered.

Meaning, like beauty, sits in the eye of the beholder.

He got to know his neighbors, who loved the city and pushed him to learn more about it. A new angle helped him see the city’s range. He could hear Burmese, Italian, Spanish, and many other languages downtown. Despite the rough edges, people worked hard to make their city better. Slowly, he started to notice and value it.

We are all small fairies sprinkling meaning wherever we go, as John Green has said.

Green saw that his boredom had nothing to do with the city and everything to do with the wrong picture he had of it. Soon he found a purpose that made him want to stay and help, building a community that eases loneliness. That idea came from a famous writer born in Indianapolis, Kurt Vonnegut.

The world always leaves room for miracles, and they can lift our hope. Many Liverpool football fans can confirm it. In the 2005 Champions League final, their team was losing three to nothing at halftime. During the break, Liverpool supporters began singing the club hymn, You’ll Never Walk Alone, and something shifted. Players fed off the song, scored three goals, and won on penalties. Liverpool grabbed one of the most famous comebacks in football history.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The Anthropocene is the current age, when the mark humans leave on the planet is huge. Human creativity in science, art, technology, and business is stunning. Yet great power carries great risk. We hear about it most in the environment, but let us be honest. The environment is only part of the story.

Could this age be the last chapter in the planet’s story? No, the planet has survived worse. About two hundred and fifty million years ago, ninety-five percent of species vanished because surface waters hit forty degrees Celsius. The Earth healed itself. Can the Anthropocene be the last chapter for humanity? Yes, it can. We hold so much power that we put many living things, including ourselves, at risk.

Seeing that should not push us into despair or worry. It should push us to be more aware of our choices and our actions. Consumer culture and rigid hierarchies bring out only parts of us, often not the best parts, like greed or envy. We also have other sides. They move us to volunteer, give to charity, protect works of art, and set up national parks. If we reflect on our actions and feed our better sides, we can save ourselves and help the planet.

Try this

Give away things you do not need and always make a shopping list to avoid buying things you do not want. Write down your daily habits and look at them. Do any of them hurt nature, and if so, how? What small steps can make your routine greener? Check the source of any story before you share it on social media. That habit cuts down on the spread of fakes.

Read it to the end?
